requirements: EO payload optics bench, thermal stability, optical baffles, focal plane interface, lens mounts
Removable payload module: optics bench, focal plane, thermal baffles, inspection window
Battery, sensor, actuator, cartridge, payload, cable, or joint modules remain removable without full teardown
Harnesses, tubes, or service channels are routed through visible raceways or protected channels
Access is represented by geometry: hatch, pocket, channel, fastener pattern, slot, window, or removable cover
Every cable exit shows strain relief geometry (clamp, grommet, tie point) and routing respects a stated minimum bend radius away from moving joints and sharp edges
Connectors are reachable without disassembling structure, have finger/tool clearance, and mention keying or labeling to prevent mis-mating
Rails, PC/104 standoffs, payload bay, and board stack clearances are visible
Avionics, battery, antenna, and payload modules have removable trays or access panels
Design is an educational structural/electrical mockup and avoids flight qualification claims
Structure, power, avionics, comms, and payload are decomposed with simple mass/volume/power budget notes per module that sum consistently

generated_parts.label: Integration Harness + Datum Spine
generated_parts.spec: Integration_harness closure spine with harness_channel, connector_keying, assembly_datum pads, routing clips, service hatch, reciprocal interface labels, inspection checklist marks

requirements: Inter-board harness routing, connectors, assembly datums, connection types and load-path closure
Battery, sensor, actuator, cartridge, payload, cable, or joint modules remain removable without full teardown.
Harnesses, tubes, or service channels are routed through visible raceways or protected channels.
Access is represented by geometry: hatch, pocket, channel, fastener pattern, slot, window, or removable cover.
Every cable exit shows strain relief geometry (clamp, grommet, tie point) and routing respects a stated minimum bend radius away from moving joints and sharp edges.
Connectors are reachable without disassembling structure, have finger/tool clearance, and mention keying or labeling to prevent mis-mating.
Primary locating faces, rail guides, axis rings, spar sockets, or mating pads are visible and labeled.
Critical slip, snap, pin, hinge, and slide interfaces state plausible clearances.
Each subsystem has at least three adjacent interfaces or datums.
Fits are named with standard intent: clearance fit for sliding, transition for locating, interference/press only where justified, with a numeric allowance.
Holes, slots, and pockets match real catalog hardware sizes (M3/M4 clearance holes, standard bearing OD, common pin diameters).
Material/process, wall assumptions, build direction, and supports are explicit enough for design review.
Verify/risk items reference real features or interfaces from part_tree/geometry_ops.
Risks cover blocked access, weak interfaces, tolerance mismatch, process limitations, and unsafe overclaiming.
Each stated requirement maps to a verification method (inspection, demonstration, analysis, test) tied to a named feature or interface.
Cross-subsystem interfaces state owner, mating geometry, and what is exchanged (load, power, data, fluid), like a miniature interface control entry.

Every inter-part interface names a specific connection type (bolted, threaded_insert, snap_fit, press_fit, dovetail_slide, clevis_pin, revolute_bearing, band_clamp, adhesive_lap, o_ring, connector_mate, harness_passthrough) with its DOF and a numeric clearance.

Parts can be installed in a stated order where every fastener, connector, and mating feature stays reachable; no installed part blocks access to a later one; last-installed and captive parts are identified.
One primary datum part anchors the assembly; each other part locates off a named parent datum using a 3-2-1 scheme, so tolerance does not accumulate uncontrolled across the stack.
Every part's primary load traces through named interfaces to the base/ground; the load path closes for all parts and high-load joints use load-appropriate connection types.

Compared to the previous version the design moves the right direction: fewer interfaces, parts consolidated where service is preserved, open load paths closed, and redundancy added only at critical joints.
The assembly uses the fewest interfaces that still allow service and manufacture; each remaining interface is justified by service, motion, manufacture split, or load transfer.

args.text: Checklist: 1. Power Distribution Board, 2. Electronics Module, 3. Payload Bay

verify.rule: cad_brief.mass_est_g must be within ±10% of 150g
Each required evidence_feature appears in part_tree/geometry_ops
Adjacent interfaces and mounting datums are named with connection_type, DOF, clearance, and required_feature
Critical joints have visible insert/washer/locking/preload evidence and torque_Nm or preload intent

risk.desc: Potential strain on harnesses due to tight routing
risk.mit: Review harness routing and ensure proper strain relief geometry

risk.desc: Incorrect connector mating due to keying issues
risk.mit: Verify connector keying during assembly and provide clear instructions

risk.desc: Inaccessible service hatch may hinder post-installation inspection
risk.mit: Ensure service hatch is clearly marked and easily accessible

risk.desc: Tolerance mismatch between aluminum and PCB components
risk.mit: Implement strict tolerance control during manufacturing

risk.desc: Overclaiming of service hatch accessibility may lead to false positives
risk.mit: Conduct thorough inspection to validate actual accessibility

_subsystem_plan.subsystem_role: Inter-board harness routing, connectors, assembly datums, connection types and load-path closure

physics_paths.path: From power distribution board to various sensors and actuators via harness_channel
From heat sinks on electronics boards to external radiator surfaces via harness_channel
Service hatch for post-installation inspection and maintenance

internal_feature_targets.description: Channel for routing cables between subsystems
Keying feature to prevent incorrect connector mating
Clip to secure harnesses and prevent movement
Hatch for post-installation inspection and maintenance

integration_opportunities: Optimize harness routing for minimal strain
Ensure all connectors are accessible for installation and removal
Provide clear visual indicators for assembly datums

manufacturing_strategy.process: CNC milling for aluminum frame, PCB fabrication for circuitry
manufacturing_strategy.datum: Primary datum face on assembly_datum pad
manufacturing_strategy.support: Support structures removed after milling, PCBs handled with ESD precautions
manufacturing_strategy.inspection: Visual inspection for alignment, functional testing for electrical integrity

verification_focus: Connector keying verification: Correct orientation and depth
Harness strain relief: Proper clamps and bend radius maintained
Service hatch accessibility: Clear opening and secure closure
